expectations.text: Advises designing around what callers actually want in their vocabulary (connect/read/write/disconnect) rather than exposing an internal concept like 'socket'
expectations.evidence: Lines 4-6 'Design around what callers actually want, in their vocabulary'. Lines 14-24 enumerate '**connect** ... **send a message** ... **receive a message** ... **disconnect**' and 'Model the surface around caller intent, not around the internal mechanism.' Lines 11-13 'A socket is a Unix *implementation* abstraction.' Direct match.

expectations.text: Warns against footgun-by-omission: sensible behavior should be the default, not gated behind obscure mandatory flags
expectations.evidence: Section line 35 '## No footgun-by-omission'. Lines 50-51 '**sensible, safe behavior is the default; deviation is an explicit opt-in.** Nothing should break because the caller didn't know to set a flag.' Concrete examples (partial send/recv, missing framing, TCP_NODELAY) at lines 40-48.

expectations.text: Recommends keeping low-level/raw bindings as a separate escape hatch so the common-case API stays simple (layered)
expectations.evidence: Section line 64 '## Layer it: keep the raw bindings as a separate escape hatch'. Lines 66-71 'Provide raw/low-level bindings ... as a **distinct lower layer** ... This is the standard layered design: make the common case simple, and the uncommon case possible.' Layer diagram at lines 73-76 (net vs net.raw).

expectations.text: Advises designing around what callers actually want in their vocabulary (connect/read/write/disconnect) rather than exposing an internal concept like 'socket'
expectations.evidence: Lines 16-25 "'Socket' is the OS's internal concept ... Exposing it makes your API a mirror of the implementation instead of a contract with the caller ... design around the *thing the caller has* (a connection) and the operations they actually perform — **connect / read / write / disconnect**." Generalizes 'not a DB mirror' to 'not an OS-internals mirror'. Genuine match.

expectations.text: Warns against footgun-by-omission: sensible behavior should be the default, not gated behind obscure mandatory flags
expectations.evidence: Section line 43 '## Make the sensible behavior the default'. Lines 54-59 'the behavior most callers need ... should be what the API gives you by default — not functionality gated behind an obscure mandatory flag the caller has to *know* to set. An API where correct behavior only happens if you remember to pass an option is a footgun.' Direct match.

expectations.text: Recommends keeping low-level/raw bindings as a separate escape hatch so the common-case API stays simple (layered)
expectations.evidence: Section line 30 '## Keep the common case simple; raw access is a lower, separate layer'. Lines 36-40 'keep it — but as a deliberately separate escape-hatch layer, not mixed into the common-case API.' Lines 66-67 'Expose raw sockets only as a separate lower layer for the rare caller who needs it.' Match.

notes: All 3 assertions pass in BOTH configs - non-discriminating. Baseline transferred REST principles (contract-not-mirror, defaults-on, layered escape hatch) cleanly to the socket question.
No discriminating assertion. Grader suggests socket-specific footguns (partial read/write loops, message framing) as a sharper test.
